text: Identifies that the father's promise is conditioned on the son's marriage to a specific person within 18 months, forming an offer capable of acceptance by performance (unilateral contract).
legal_basis: Contract formation requires offer and acceptance; father's conditional promise and son's marriage within 18 months constitute offer and acceptance.

text: Recognizes the issue of whether the son's marriage constitutes bargained-for consideration or is merely a condition on a gratuitous promise.
legal_basis: Consideration requires a bargained-for exchange; son's prior intent to marry weakens inducement but does not automatically negate consideration.

text: States that the Statute of Frauds applies to contracts made upon consideration of marriage other than mutual promises to marry, requiring a signed writing for enforceability.
legal_basis: Statute of Frauds bars enforcement of marriage-consideration contracts without signed writing.

text: Explains that the father's oral promise falls within the marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds category and is unenforceable due to lack of a signed writing.
legal_basis: Promise conditioned on marriage to specific person within 18 months with no writing is unenforceable under Statute of Frauds.

text: Correctly applies the one-year Statute of Frauds provision and concludes it does not bar enforcement because performance was possible within one year.
legal_basis: One-year provision applies only if contract cannot be performed within one year; here, marriage could have occurred within one year.

text: Identifies the suretyship Statute of Frauds issue and explains why it likely does not apply because the promise was made to the son (debtor), not the creditor.
legal_basis: Suretyship writing requirement applies to promises made to creditor; promise to debtor weakens application.

text: Discusses the main purpose (leading object) exception to the suretyship writing requirement and explains why it does not overcome the marriage-consideration writing requirement here.
legal_basis: Main purpose exception may remove suretyship writing requirement but does not affect separate marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds.

text: Evaluates the possibility of promissory estoppel enforcing the oral promise and correctly notes its weakness due to the son's prior intent and courts' caution in overriding Statute of Frauds.
legal_basis: Promissory estoppel requires inducement and reliance; weak inducement and SoF caution limit its applicability.

text: Explains that the father's unilateral mistake about the availability of a tax deduction does not excuse performance absent an express condition.
legal_basis: Unilateral mistake about expected benefit generally does not excuse contractual performance.

text: Concludes that the father's oral promise is unenforceable due to the Statute of Frauds marriage-consideration writing requirement and absence of a signed writing.
legal_basis: Marriage-consideration contracts require signed writing; none exists here, so promise is unenforceable.

text: Avoids the material doctrinal error of treating the son's prior intent to marry as automatically negating consideration.
legal_basis: Prior intent does not automatically defeat consideration if the act is demanded as exchange.

text: Avoids the error of ignoring the Statute of Frauds writing requirement for contracts in consideration of marriage.
legal_basis: Writing requirement is dispositive for marriage-consideration contracts.

text: Correctly distinguishes between the suretyship and marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds provisions and does not conflate their requirements.
legal_basis: Suretyship and marriage-consideration are separate SoF categories with independent writing requirements.

text: Includes analysis of the father's motivation (tax deduction) and its limited legal effect on enforceability.
legal_basis: Motivation for promise is relevant to main purpose exception but does not excuse performance due to mistake.

text: Identifies the issue of enforceability of the father's oral promise as the central legal question.
legal_basis: Enforceability depends on contract formation, Statute of Frauds, and exceptions.

text: Demonstrates understanding that full performance (marriage) does not remove the Statute of Frauds writing requirement for marriage-consideration contracts.
legal_basis: Performance does not satisfy writing requirement absent recognized substitute doctrine.
